content.problemStatement:
Excellent work with all the analysis.
- Cheese and Pepperoni pizzas have the highest average delay. \
What is the process of at the outlet that causes a higher delay specifically for these 2 pizza types? 
- There seems to have been some problem on 4th, 5th, 6th April.\
Time to call up the Outlet manager, talk to his other employees, check the CCTV footage and attendance register

Remember - data analysis is just a way to identify the cause. \
To develop great analytical skills - you will always need an understanding of operations / process / business as may be applicable. Remember some risks of getting lost in the data
- Confirmation Bias: One of the risks of getting lost in data is that you may become so focused on the data that you overlook important information or data that contradicts your initial assumptions. This can lead to confirmation bias, where you only see what you want to see in the data, and overlook or dismiss any information that does not fit your preconceived notions.
- Lack of Context: Another risk of getting lost in data is that you may lose sight of the context in which the data was collected. Without understanding the context, it's easy to draw the wrong conclusions from the data or make decisions based on incomplete or inaccurate information.
- Missing the Big Picture: Data can provide a lot of valuable insights, but it's important to remember that it's just one piece of the puzzle. By getting too caught up in the data, you may miss the big picture or overlook other important factors that can influence your decisions.

Click on 'Next' to proceed to the Intermediate level case study.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
